title: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
author: brettbaggett77@gmail.com
orientation: A direct exhortation to Christian men, citing Athanasius and Luther as historical examples of solitary courage, to repent of pro-life compromise and demand the total, immediate abolition of abortion. The author confesses his own former support of Heartbeat Bills as cowardice and calls men to stand for preborn neighbors politically, at abortion mills, and against worldly opposition.
audiences: Christian men currently supporting pro-life incremental legislation
Pastors and church leaders weighing whether to back Heartbeat Bills
State legislators considering abolition vs. regulation bills
Abolitionist activists looking for rhetorical and theological framing
Skeptics who view abolitionists as needlessly divisive

questions.question: Why does the author argue that Heartbeat Bills are not a faithful step toward abolishing abortion?
questions.audience: Christian men currently supporting pro-life incremental legislation
questions.answer: Heartbeat Bills, in the author's view, simply set a window during which abortion remains legal, violate biblical impartiality and Constitutional equal protection, and submit to a federal judiciary that will block them. They regulate murder rather than prohibit it.
questions.quote: This includes Heartbeat Bills, which simply tells people to commit murder before the abortionist detects a heartbeat; which violates the Biblical commands for impartiality in the courts and the rescue of those being led to slaughter; which violates the Constitutional mandates of equal protection and the right to life; which submit to the federal judiciary and thereby will never go into effect.

questions.question: How does the author use Romans 1 to indict men who back pro-life legislation?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders weighing whether to back Heartbeat Bills
questions.answer: He applies Paul's condemnation of those who 'give approval to those that do such things' to anyone supporting laws that license murder under any conditions, treating such approval as participation in the sin itself.
questions.quote: Toward the end of Romans 1, when Paul is describing unrighteousness that is characteristic of idolators who are under "the wrath of God" and who have therefore been "given up to a debased mind," he says they not only "murder" but they "give approval to those that do such things."

questions.question: What personal example of repentance does Brett Baggett offer his readers?
questions.audience: Christian men currently supporting pro-life incremental legislation
questions.answer: He admits he once supported Heartbeat Bills out of willful ignorance and compromise, calls that a failure of courage, and publicly repents while inviting other men to do the same.
questions.quote: I once supported Heartbeat Bills due to my willful ignorance. I was wrong. I compromised. I thought "this is as good as we can get." This is not how courageous men act. Courageous men investigate the truth and stand boldly for what is righteous. I had to repent. Will you repent from any apathy or compromise with me?

questions.question: Why are Athanasius and Martin Luther invoked as patterns for present-day abolitionists?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists looking for rhetorical and theological framing
questions.answer: Both stood essentially alone against the prevailing powers of their day to defend doctrines they believed Scripture required. The author argues that if they could stand alone for doctrine, modern men should at least stand together to defend preborn children's lives.
questions.quote: If these men stood *alone* to defend the precious doctrines of Christ and His Word, then what kind of cowards would we be if we, in this day, did not stand *together* to defend the lives of precious children as they are led to the slaughter by the millions?

questions.question: What does the author say civil government is positively obligated to do about abortion?
questions.audience: State legislators considering abolition vs. regulation bills
questions.answer: Citing Romans 13 and the Mosaic homicide statutes, he argues government must protect life by criminalizing the intentional killing of a human being without exception, applying the same homicide laws inside the womb as outside.
questions.quote: Men, it is time for you to demand the government to do what God has ordained it to do — namely, to protect life by opposing evil and rewarding good (Romans 13:1-4). It is time for abortion — the intentional killing of a human being — to be abolished (Psalm 82:2-4)! It must be illegal to murder a human being, period (Leviticus 20:1-5). The same homicide laws that protect you outside of the womb must protect those inside (Exodus 22:21-24).

questions.question: How does the author frame the binary choice he wants every man to make?
questions.audience: Skeptics who view abolitionists as needlessly divisive
questions.answer: He repeatedly poses the question as a stark either/or: cowardly boy approving of murder, or godly man rescuing the fatherless like Job. The framing is intentionally polarizing because he believes neutrality functions as approval.
questions.quote: Will you act like a cowardly boy in the midst of this abortion holocaust or will you act like a courageous man to love your preborn neighbors?

questions.question: Beyond legislation, what concrete actions does the author urge men to take?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists looking for rhetorical and theological framing
questions.answer: He calls men to stand outside abortion mills, preach the gospel, offer spiritual and material help to mothers, and accept opposition from the world rather than seeking man's approval.
questions.quote: Will you stand for your preborn neighbors not only politically but also outside of abortion mills, preaching the gospel and offering spiritual and material help? Will you stand against the world if you have to because you are more concerned with pleasing God than man (Galatians 1:10)?

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Extends the same argument that pro-life incrementalism is a moral compromise that betrays preborn children.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: A concrete Oklahoma case study of the political cowardice this article condemns in the abstract.

related.title: Christian, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Develops the theological case for repenting of the pro-life label and embracing abolition, the same call to repentance Baggett models here.

related.title: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
related.reason: Equips the courageous men this article addresses with answers to the standard legislative pushback against abolition bills.

related.title: Incremental Laws of All Sorts Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Reinforces the critique of Heartbeat Bills and other incremental measures as obstacles to repentance and true abolition.

related.title: Why So Vehement, So Unyielding, So Severe?
related.reason: Defends the uncompromising tone Baggett uses, answering objections that abolitionist rhetoric is too harsh.
